short_description: Continuous performance task requiring online maintenance and updating of stimulus identity across N intervening items.
long_description: The N-Back Working Memory (NBACK) paradigm is among the most widely used measures of working memory capacity and updating in cognitive neuroscience. Participants monitor a continuous stream of stimuli and indicate whether each item matches the one presented N positions back in the sequence. The critical manipulation — varying N — parametrically increases the demand on active maintenance and interference resolution. The 2-back condition (N=2) contrasts with a 0-back baseline (target defined by identity alone), isolating working memory updating from simple target detection.
background: Working memory — the capacity to hold and manipulate information over short intervals — is one of the best predictors of fluid intelligence and academic achievement. The N-back task has been shown to activate a frontoparietal network including dorsolateral prefrontal cortex, posterior parietal cortex, and anterior cingulate, and has been used extensively to study individual differences, ageing, pharmacological modulation, and cognitive training effects. The 2-back versus 0-back contrast is one of the most reliably reproduced contrasts in the fMRI literature, making NBACK a useful functional localiser as well as a standalone measure.
procedure: Each session consists of four blocks of 2-back and two blocks of 0-back stimuli. Stimuli are letters (or spatial positions) presented at a 2-s rate. Participants press a target key (match) or a non-target key (no match) for each item. Accuracy and RT are scored per block. Block order is counterbalanced.
trial_structure: 2-back trial: letter displayed for 500 ms, 1500 ms ISI; respond if letter matches 2 positions back. 0-back trial: identical timing; respond if letter matches a pre-specified target (e.g., 'X'). Each block: 40 items (2-back) or 20 items (0-back), ~25% targets.
design: Within-subjects, two-session repeated-measures. Four blocks of 2-back and two of 0-back per session.
